unitTitle: KS1 Y1 Moving a robot (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Buttons — what each command does
lessons.objectives:
I can predict the outcome of a command on a device
I can match a command to an outcome
I can run a command on a device
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: forwards · backwards · turn · clear · go · commands. TA: one floor robot between two; the starter worksheet open; everyone has the seven-button sheet for Activity 2. Read the 3 "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what is a robot? A robot is a machine that does a job because a human programmed it. Pupils CHOOSE why a floor robot moves on the starter worksheet.
2. (5–10) The buttons. Show the robot AND the button image. Pupils LABEL the buttons (drag Forwards/Backwards/Left turn/Right turn/Go/Clear onto the diagram). Likely error: ignoring Clear (X) → fix-words "Clear first, every time."
   • Support: matching — button to its job.  • Core: choose which button runs / clears.  • Challenge: multi-select ALL the direction command buttons.
3. (10–30) I-do → we-do → you-do. Demo Clear → Forwards → (predict) → Go. Then pupils try one button at a time (Clear in between). TA: prompt, do not press it for them.
4. (30–45) Activity 2 — match movement to button. Teacher secretly presses one button, runs it; pupils point to the button on their sheet (the activity worksheet matching task). Move round the circle so everyone gets a turn behind the robot.
   • Support: match the move to the button.  • Core: which button to press first / what Go does.  • Challenge: a button that made it NOT move, and why.
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the 3 "I can…"; each pupil names a button and what it does.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody's screen looks "less done". No flashing; robot sound off if it suits; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Floor robot buttons (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Directions — giving clear instructions
lessons.objectives:
I can follow an instruction
I can recall words that can be acted out
I can give directions
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: instructions · directions. TA: space to move safely; the floor robot ready for the link-back; the starter worksheet open. Read the 3 "I can…" aloud. (This lesson is unplugged — pupils act like the robot.)
1. (0–5) Starter — last lesson the buttons gave the robot instructions. Pupils CHOOSE what the buttons give the robot.
2. (5–10) Activity 1 — you are the robot. Teacher gives one tiny instruction at a time (stand up, nod, clap once); pupils do ONLY that. Likely error: adding extra actions → fix-words "only do what I said."
3. (10–20) Activity 2 — words we can act out. Build a class list with a small picture beside each word. Pupils SORT instructions into "can act it out" vs "too big to act out" on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: sort the instructions.  • Core: choose the clearer instruction (step vs walk).  • Challenge: explain why "walk" is a bad robot instruction.
4. (20–35) Activity 3 — give directions. In pairs, one gives clear instructions, one follows (the activity worksheet: order the instructions, then write three). Allow "turn" + point if left/right is hard.
   • Support: order the instructions.  • Core: the robot does only what it is told / which word moves one step.  • Challenge: write three instructions from door to chair.
5. (35–40) Plenary — self-tick the 3 "I can…"; each pupil gives one clear instruction.
Differentiation on the SAME task; plain literal language; movement is the activity, not a reward.

lessons.title: Forwards and backwards — making a sequence
lessons.objectives:
I can compare forward and backward movements
I can start a sequence from the same place
I can predict the outcome of a sequence involving forwards and backwards commands
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: forwards · backwards · commands. TA: one robot between two; an A3 mat per group; the paper-bot and the activity worksheet ready. Read the 3 "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — which buttons today? Show the side-view robot. Pupils CHOOSE the buttons needed (Forwards, Backwards, Clear, Go) and SORT commands into moves-forwards / moves-backwards / does-not-move on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: sort the commands.  • Core: predict the stop square for Forwards, Forwards.  • Challenge: explain why the same start square matters.
2. (5–15) Activity 1 — start and forwards. Everyone starts on the SAME square. One Forwards = one square. Two Forwards = two squares. Likely error: starting anywhere → fix-words "same square, every time."
3. (15–25) Activities 2–3 — backwards, then mixing. One Backwards = one square back. Try Forwards then Backwards (returns to start). Clear between every program.
4. (25–45) Activity 4 — where will it get to? Pupils put the robot on the start square, read a program, mark their prediction, then run it (the activity worksheet: order the run-a-program steps, then predict). Paper-bot to step through by hand first.
   • Support: order the steps to run a program.  • Core: predict the stop square for three-command programs.  • Challenge: write a program that lands on a chosen square.
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the 3 "I can…"; each pupil shows a program they predicted correctly.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Floor robot from the side (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Four directions — turns and moves together
lessons.objectives:
I can compare left and right turns
I can experiment with turn and move commands to move a robot
I can predict the outcome of a sequence involving up to four commands
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: left · right · turn · commands. TA: one robot between two; mats and paper-bots from Lesson 3; the starter worksheet open. Read the 3 "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — the turn buttons. Show the button image. Pupils CHOOSE what a turn command does (turns on the spot) and SORT commands into moves-to-a-new-square vs turns-on-the-spot.
   • Support: sort the commands.  • Core: choose that left and right turn the same amount.  • Challenge: explain why we use turns at all.
2. (5–15) Activity 1 — in a spin. Try each turn button (Clear between). The robot turns ON THE SPOT, same amount left or right. Likely error: thinking a turn also moves a square → fix-words "a turn changes which way it faces, not which square."
3. (15–30) Activity 2 — challenge maps. Trial and error: program the robot from start to finish square, always starting facing the top of the mat. Discourage picking the robot up.
4. (30–45) Activity 3 — work it out. Pupils step through six programs and predict the finish square (the activity worksheet: order a four-command program, then predict).
   • Support: order the four commands.  • Core: which way it faces after a turn / how many moves.  • Challenge: write a four-command program.
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the 3 "I can…"; each pupil shows a turn and a move. Confidence check.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Floor robot buttons (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Getting there — plan, program and debug
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what my program should do
I can choose the order of commands in a sequence
I can debug my program
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: plan · algorithm · program. TA: one robot between two; destination cards (cut up), command cards, mat and paper-bot ready; the starter worksheet open. Read the 3 "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — words for today. An algorithm is the ordered plan; pressing the buttons makes it a program. Pupils CHOOSE the meaning of algorithm and MATCH algorithm / program / debug to their meanings.
   • Support: match the three words.  • Core: choose what to do first when it goes wrong.  • Challenge: explain what "debug" means.
2. (5–15) Activity 1 — getting from here to there. Turn over a start card and a destination card; place the paper-bot; POINT OUT a route first (design before code). No robot needed yet.
3. (15–45) Activity 2 — plan, program, debug. Choose command cards for the route and put them in order (forwards/backwards between squares; turns inside a square) = the algorithm. Press the buttons in the same order = the program. Likely error: turn cards between squares → fix-words "turns stay inside a square."
   • Support: order the make-a-program steps.  • Core: when to plan / fix a one-square-short program.  • Challenge: describe finding and fixing a wrong-turn bug.
4. (45–50) Plenary — fixing a program is called debugging. Self-tick the 3 "I can…"; each pupil shows a program they planned and tested. Confidence check.
Differentiation on the SAME task; TA: prompt, do not do it for them; no flashing; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Routes — more than one solution
lessons.objectives:
I can identify several possible solutions
I can plan two programs
I can use two different programs to get to the same place
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: route · plan · program. TA: one robot between two; A3 story mat, destination cards and the Route planner sheet; command cards from Lesson 5; the starter worksheet open. Read the 3 "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — more than one way. There is often more than one route to the same place. Pupils CHOOSE that there is more than one route and SORT true / not-true facts about routes.
   • Support: sort the facts.  • Core: choose that both programs start the same square, facing the same way.  • Challenge: explain why two different programs can both be good answers.
2. (5–10) Activity 1 — this way and that way. In pairs, draw two or three routes between the SAME start and end squares on the planner. No robot yet — this is design.
3. (10–30) Activity 2 — design your programs. Choose the start facing; make an algorithm for each route with command cards; test and debug each. Both start from the same square, facing the same way (the activity worksheet: order the plan-and-test steps, then the final challenge). Likely error: changing the start facing between the two → fix-words "same start, same facing, both times."
   • Support: order the plan-and-test steps.  • Core: what to do for the second route / same start.  • Challenge: plan the final route using only Left turns and Forwards.
4. (30–40) Activity 3 + plenary — longer routes, going back over squares. Self-tick the 3 "I can…"; each pupil shows two programs that reach the same place. Confidence check — end of unit.
Differentiation on the SAME task; calm independent work; TA: prompt, do not do it for them; no flashing; movement breaks are routine.
